title: Chaos and Complexity in Cancer
text:
Overview
Cancer is often described as a disease of increasing disorder. This project tests whether that intuition is actually correct.
This site presents a reconstruction and extension of my doctoral dissertation, Chaos and Complexity in Cancer (George Mason University, 2004), using a computational framework that treats cancer as a dynamical system.
Rather than assuming uniform increases in randomness, the analysis examines how complexity, entropy, and regulatory structure are reorganized as tissue transitions from normal to malignant states.
Across cancer types, the results suggest a recurring pattern of regulatory simplification accompanied by selective consolidation of functional pathways, rather than simple increases in disorder.
crumbs: 1  Chaos and Complexity in Cancer

title: Chaos and Complexity in Cancer
text:
Central Question

How is biological complexity reorganized during tumorigenesis?

This project evaluates whether cancer exhibits:

chaotic dynamics (increased heterogeneity and disorder)

anti-chaotic dynamics (constraint, clonality, reduced variability)

or a context-dependent interplay between both
crumbs: 1  Chaos and Complexity in Cancer

title: Chaos and Complexity in Cancer
text:
Example: BLAD/TCC
A representative comparison (bladder normal vs transitional cell carcinoma) illustrates the type of structure detected.

Complexity: net decrease with localized functional gains

Entropy: heterogeneous (coexisting chaotic and anti-chaotic behavior)

Structure: selective organization in functional pathways


Tumorigenesis appears as a reorganization of biological complexity,
involving loss of regulatory flexibility alongside
consolidation of functional and process-specific pathways.

title: Chaos and Complexity in Cancer
text:
Conceptual Perspective
Cells are modeled as high-dimensional dynamical systems with:

nonlinear regulatory interactions

multiple attractor states

sensitivity to perturbation

Within this framework, cancer is interpreted as a state transition process in gene regulatory space.
Rather than uniform chaos, different cancers exhibit different balances of:

destabilization (chaotic behavior)

constraint (anti-chaotic, structured states)

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.1 Introduction
Cancer is traditionally described in terms of genetic mutations and pathway disruptions. However, this perspective does not fully capture the system-level transformations that occur during tumorigenesis. An alternative view treats cancer as a transformation in the organization of cellular systems.
This work adopts a systems-level perspective in which normal and tumor tissues are understood as distinct configurations of a high-dimensional biological state space. The central question is not only which genes change, but how the structure of gene expression changes.

title: 2  Overview
section: 2.2 Cancer as a Transformation of Organization
text:
2.2 Cancer as a Transformation of Organization
Let \(X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}\) denote a gene expression matrix. Each sample corresponds to a point in a high-dimensional space. Normal and tumor tissues can therefore be interpreted as distributions within this space.
Tumorigenesis can be viewed as a transformation:
\[
X_{\text{normal}} \rightarrow X_{\text{tumor}}
\]
This transformation may involve:

changes in dimensionality

changes in dispersion

changes in structure and predictability

These changes motivate the need for quantitative measures of organization.

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.3 From Genes to Structure
Rather than focusing on individual genes, this work focuses on:

the distribution of variance across dimensions

the structure of covariance

the geometry of sample distributions

These properties can be quantified using eigenvalue-based measures and geometric representations.

title: 2  Overview
text:
2.4 Scope
The following chapters develop:

A theoretical basis for viewing the cell as a complex system

A conceptualization of cancer as a transformation within that system

A summary linking these ideas to measurable quantities

Subsequent sections translate these concepts into statistical, geometric, and interpretive frameworks.

title: 3  The Cell as a Complex System
text:
3.1 The Cell as a High-Dimensional System
A cell is a dynamical system composed of interacting molecular components. Gene expression profiles provide a measurable projection of this system, capturing coordinated activity across thousands of genes.
Each cell state can be represented as a vector:
\[
x \in \mathbb{R}^p
\]
where \(p\) is the number of measured transcripts.
crumbs: 3  The Cell as a Complex System

title: 3  The Cell as a Complex System
text:
3.2 Degrees of Freedom
The effective number of independent degrees of freedom in this system is typically much lower than \(p\), due to regulatory coupling. This reduction is reflected in the covariance structure of the expression matrix.
Let \(\Sigma\) denote the covariance matrix. Its eigenvalues \(\lambda_i\) describe how variance is distributed across orthogonal directions.
The distribution of these eigenvalues encodes:

dimensionality

redundancy

coordination
crumbs: 3  The Cell as a Complex System

title: 3  The Cell as a Complex System
text:
3.3 Complexity and Constraint
Biological systems balance:

complexity — multiple active regulatory pathways

constraint — coordination and control

This balance is reflected in the spectrum of \(\Sigma\):

distributed eigenvalues → higher complexity

concentrated eigenvalues → stronger constraint

These concepts form the basis for the quantitative measures introduced later.
crumbs: 3  The Cell as a Complex System

title: 3  The Cell as a Complex System
text:
3.4 Implications for Measurement
Because the system is high-dimensional and structured, meaningful analysis requires:

dimensionality-sensitive measures

entropy-based measures

geometric representations

These tools allow us to quantify how cellular organization changes between normal and tumor states.
crumbs: 3  The Cell as a Complex System

title: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System
text:
4.1 Cancer as System Transformation
Cancer can be viewed as a transformation of the cellular system rather than a collection of isolated mutations. This transformation affects the structure of gene expression and the relationships among genes.
crumbs: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System

title: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System
text:
4.2 Adaptive and Nonlinear Behavior
Tumor systems exhibit features consistent with complex adaptive systems:

nonlinear interactions

feedback regulation

adaptation to environmental pressures

These properties can produce diverse outcomes, including both increased disorder and increased constraint.
crumbs: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System

title: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System
text:
4.3 Variability Across Tumors
Not all tumors exhibit the same structural changes. Some may show:

increased heterogeneity

expansion into new expression states

Others may show:

reduced variability

convergence toward constrained states

This variability motivates a framework capable of distinguishing multiple modes of transformation.
crumbs: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System

title: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System
text:
4.4 From Dynamics to Observables
While the underlying system is dynamic, gene expression data provide cross-sectional observations. Therefore, we do not directly observe trajectories, but rather distributions of states.
The analysis must therefore focus on:

structure of distributions

geometry of sample clouds

variance across dimensions

These observable properties provide indirect insight into system organization.
crumbs: 4  Cancer as a Complex Adaptive System

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.1 Summary of Theoretical Perspective
The preceding chapters establish the following:
These ideas motivate a shift from gene-level analysis to system-level analysis.
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
The cell is a high-dimensional system with structured variability

Gene expression provides a measurable representation of this system

Cancer represents a transformation in system organization

Different tumors may exhibit distinct modes of transformation
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.2 From Concepts to Quantities
To evaluate these ideas empirically, we require quantitative measures of:

dimensionality

dispersion

predictability

structure

These are derived from the covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) and its eigenvalues \(\lambda_i\).
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.3 Core Quantities
The following quantities are used:

participation ratio (effective dimensionality)

entropy of eigenvalue distribution

effective rank

anisotropy

These measures quantify how variance is distributed across dimensions.
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.4 Geometric Interpretation
These statistical quantities have geometric interpretations:

higher dimensionality → broader occupation of state space

lower dimensionality → collapse onto fewer directions

higher entropy → diffuse distributions

lower entropy → concentrated distributions

Principal component analysis provides a linear geometric representation of these properties, while latent-space methods extend this representation to nonlinear embeddings.
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.5 Transition to Methods
The next chapters operationalize these concepts.
First, complexity and entropy are quantified using eigenvalue-based measures derived from expression data. Next, PCA provides a linear geometric projection of these structures. Finally, variational autoencoders construct nonlinear latent representations for deeper geometric analysis.
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework
text:
5.6 Transition to Interpretive Framework
The final step is to map these quantitative and geometric observations to biologically meaningful regimes.
This mapping is formalized in the framework chapter, which defines categories such as complexity gain, complexity loss, entropy gain, and entropy loss, and relates them to latent-space transformations.
crumbs: 5  Theory Summary and Transition to Quantitative Framework

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
6.1 Overview
This study utilizes the gene expression dataset introduced by Ramaswamy et al. (2001), one of the earliest large-scale efforts to construct a unified molecular classification framework across multiple human malignancies. The dataset was designed to evaluate whether tumors could be classified based solely on gene expression profiles, independent of traditional clinical and histopathological features.
The dataset comprises oligonucleotide microarray measurements of 16,063 probe sets, representing approximately 14,000 genes and expressed sequence tags. Samples were collected from multiple institutional tumor banks and subjected to centralized pathological review to confirm tissue of origin and histological classification.
Following quality control procedures, the final dataset consists of:
Samples failing quality control were excluded based on criteria including RNA degradation, abnormal chip intensity, low detection rates (“present” calls), and visible microarray artifacts. These procedures ensured that downstream analyses were performed on technically reliable expression profiles.

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
218 tumor samples spanning 14 distinct cancer classes

90 normal tissue samples

Approximately 5 million gene expression measurements

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
6.2 Data Acquisition and Microarray Platforms
Gene expression profiling was performed using two Affymetrix microarray platforms:

Hu6800 GeneChip
Hu35KsubA GeneChip

Each probe set was treated as an independent feature, and expression values were computed using Affymetrix GeneChip analysis software. The use of two platforms introduces heterogeneity in probe representation and coverage, which has implications for downstream preprocessing, filtering, and integration steps.
All tumor samples were derived from primary sites (except where explicitly noted), obtained prior to treatment, and enriched for malignant cells (>50%). Normal tissue RNA was obtained from snap-frozen autopsy specimens. Tissue acquisition followed institutional review protocols and was conducted using anonymized discarded tissue.

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
6.3 Original Analytical Framework
In the original study, gene expression data were subjected to a variation-based filtering procedure designed to retain genes exhibiting substantial dynamic range across samples. Specifically, genes were retained if they demonstrated at least a 5-fold and 500-unit variation after thresholding and ceiling constraints were applied. This resulted in a filtered set of 11,322 genes used for clustering and classification.
The dataset was then standardized by mean-centering and variance scaling each gene. Both unsupervised and supervised learning approaches were applied:

Unsupervised methods (hierarchical clustering and self-organizing maps) revealed partial structure but failed to cleanly separate all tumor classes.
Supervised learning, using a support vector machine (SVM) with a one-versus-all (OVA) classification scheme, achieved approximately 78% classification accuracy across tumor types.

Importantly, the study demonstrated that:

Tumor classification relies on distributed, high-dimensional gene expression patterns, rather than a small number of markers

Many tumors retain strong tissue-of-origin signals at the molecular level

Poorly differentiated tumors exhibit distinct and less classifiable expression profiles, suggesting fundamentally altered regulatory programs

These findings established the dataset as a foundational reference for molecular cancer classification.

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
6.4 Adaptation in the Present Study
In the present work, the Ramaswamy dataset serves as the input to a modular computational pipeline consisting of preprocessing, analysis, and reporting stages. While the original dataset and its structure are preserved, several methodological extensions are introduced:

Robust normalization using RMA (Robust Multi-array Average)

Flexible gene filtering strategies, including:

variance-based filtering

differential expression filtering (limma)


Integration of biological annotation layers:

Gene Ontology (GO)

KEGG pathways

MSigDB gene sets


Extension to latent-space modeling using variational autoencoders (VAEs)

These adaptations allow the dataset to be analyzed beyond classification accuracy, enabling investigation of:

Complexity and entropy of gene expression structure

Latent geometric organization of samples

Biologically interpretable regulatory patterns associated with tumorigenesis

In particular, the transition from normal to tumor states is examined not only in terms of differential expression, but also in terms of structural reorganization in high-dimensional and latent spaces, providing a complementary perspective on cancer as a dynamical system.

title: 6  Dataset Description
text:
6.5 Dataset Selection Rationale
Although more recent gene expression datasets are available—particularly those generated using next-generation sequencing technologies such as RNA sequencing (RNA-seq), which provide higher resolution and broader dynamic range—a deliberate decision was made to retain the Ramaswamy dataset for the present study.
This choice reflects both practical and methodological considerations. The computational pipelines developed in earlier work are tightly coupled to the structure and characteristics of this dataset, and extending these pipelines to incorporate latent-space modeling (e.g., variational autoencoders) was more tractable than undertaking a complete refactoring for a modern dataset at this stage.
Importantly, the pipeline architecture is highly modular. This design allows for future substitution of the underlying dataset with minimal disruption to downstream analytical components. Accordingly, incorporation of contemporary RNA-seq–based datasets is anticipated in future work, enabling validation and extension of the present findings within a more modern experimental framework.

title: 7  Preprocessing Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Data
text:
Overview
This document describes the preprocessing pipeline used to construct cleaned, chip-specific ExpressionSet objects from raw Affymetrix CEL files for the Ramaswamy global cancer dataset, represented in GEO as GSE68928. The pipeline now also exports machine-learning-ready expression and metadata files used by the downstream latent-space notebooks.
The preprocessing pipeline operates on the Ramaswamy et al. dataset described in the previous section, including its dual-platform microarray structure (Affymetrix Hu6800 and Hu35KsubA). As such, preprocessing must account for platform-specific probe representations, metadata inconsistencies, and quality-controlled sample inclusion.
Driver script: scripts/preprocessing/01_run_preprocessing_pipeline.R
Configuration file: R/config/global_cancer/preprocessing_config.R
Estimated runtime:
Primary ExpressionSet outputs:
Primary ML / latent-space outputs:
Primary log file:
The goal of this preprocessing pipeline is to transform raw public microarray data into normalized, metadata-enriched ExpressionSet objects suitable for downstream analysis of cancer expression structure, complexity, entropy, and latent-space geometry. The latent-space notebooks do not process raw ExpressionSet objects directly; instead, they consume the canonical CSV files exported by the final preprocessing stage.

title: 7  Preprocessing Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Data
text:
Historical and Methodological Context
The original version of this research was implemented using a text-based data pipeline. Microarray data were processed as flat files, stored in a relational database, and queried to generate analysis-ready subsets.
In the current reconstruction, this workflow has been translated into a Bioconductor-centered architecture based on chip-specific ExpressionSet objects. This introduces a clean separation among:

raw expression data derived directly from CEL files via RMA;
phenotype metadata retrieved from GEO and attached post hoc;
biological annotation layers computed at the full-chip level; and
exported machine-learning inputs used by Python/Jupyter workflows.

Because public GEO metadata are not always perfectly aligned with the CEL-derived sample structure—and because the metadata include spelling, formatting, and labeling inconsistencies—a dedicated metadata attachment and cleaning stage is required.
The preprocessing pipeline therefore serves as the bridge between the original data-engineering architecture, the modern Bioconductor representation, and the latent-space analysis pipeline.
It should be noted that this preprocessing framework differs from the original analytical workflow of Ramaswamy et al. (2001), which employed variation-based gene filtering and standardized expression values prior to classification. In contrast, the present pipeline applies RMA normalization and defers feature selection to downstream analysis stages, enabling flexible comparison between filtering strategies.

title: 7  Preprocessing Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Data
text:
Pipeline Controls
The pipeline is controlled through preprocessing_config.R. The principal toggles are:







Toggle
Purpose




download_enabled
Download CEL files from FTP if needed


build_esets
Build chip-specific ExpressionSet objects from CEL files


process_metadata
Attach, clean, and label GEO phenotype metadata


run_annotation
Annotate all probes for each supported chip


export_ml_inputs
Export machine-learning / latent-space input files


run_ml_pca_check
Produce a PCA sanity-check plot from the exported ML matrix



The current latent-space configuration uses:
ml_chip_id       <- "hu35ksuba"
ml_filter_method <- "variance"
ml_top_n         <- 3000
This means the final export stage writes a full expression matrix and a variance-filtered top-3000-probe matrix for the hu35ksuba platform. The design can later be generalized to both chips by iterating over names(geo_chip_map).

title: 7  Preprocessing Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Data
text:
Workflow Steps

1. Download .CEL Files (Optional)
Purpose: Retrieve raw Affymetrix CEL files if not already available locally.

Controlled by: download_enabled
Script: R/preprocessing/download_cel_files.R
Source: EBI FTP (E-GEOD-68928)
Destination: data/global_cancer/CEL/

Notes:
In the current workflow, downloads are usually disabled because CEL files are already present locally. Download behavior can be tested conservatively by using dry_run and download_limit before enabling a full retrieval.



2. Build ExpressionSet Objects
Purpose: Detect chip type, validate CEL files, group samples by platform, and normalize each chip-specific group using RMA.

Controlled by: build_esets
Script: R/preprocessing/build_expression_sets.R

Chip/platform map:



Internal chip key
GEO platform




hu35ksuba
GPL98


hu6800
GPL80



Behavior:

Detect chip type using Affymetrix metadata
Skip unreadable or invalid CEL files, with skipped files logged
Group valid CEL files by chip
Normalize each chip-specific group using RMA
Return a named list of chip-specific ExpressionSet objects

Output:

eset_list, a named list of chip-specific ExpressionSet objects

Because the dataset spans two microarray platforms with partially distinct probe sets, all preprocessing steps are performed at the chip level. This avoids introducing artifacts from probe mismatch and preserves a consistent feature space within each platform for downstream analysis.



3. Attach and Clean GEO Metadata
Accurate alignment between expression data and phenotype metadata is a critical requirement for all downstream analyses. This step ensures that biological labels such as tissue type and disease state are correctly associated with each expression profile, forming the basis for both statistical comparisons and latent-space modeling.
Purpose: Attach phenotype metadata from GEO and convert free-text fields into analysis-ready labels.

Controlled by: process_metadata
Scripts:

R/preprocessing/attach_geo_metadata.R
R/helpers/clean_and_label_metadata.R

GEO accession: GSE68928

Attachment behavior:

Load locally available GEO metadata files
Map GEO platforms to internal chip names
Match samples using GSM identifiers derived from CEL filenames
Write matched phenotype data into pData(eset)

Cleaning behavior:

Correct known tissue-label and disease-label inconsistencies
Standardize disease and tissue metadata fields
Derive condition as normal or cancer
Derive tissue_label for downstream grouping

Important alignment convention:
Expression columns often use CEL-derived names such as GSM1686773_CL2000062805AA.CEL, whereas GEO metadata are keyed by GSM accession such as GSM1686773. The pipeline therefore matches samples using the GSM prefix and then restores the CEL-derived sample IDs for downstream consistency.



4. Annotate Full-Chip Probe Sets
Purpose: Attach biological annotation to all probes on each supported chip.

Controlled by: run_annotation
Scripts:

R/wrappers/run_annotate_chip_probes.R
R/annotate/annotate_chip_probes.R


Annotation dimensions:

Gene symbols (SYMBOL)
Gene Ontology terms, including Molecular Function and Biological Process
KEGG pathways
MSigDB Hallmark gene sets

Output:

output/global_cancer/RData/annotations/full_chip_annotations.rds

Notes:
Annotation is performed on the full chip once during preprocessing. Downstream analysis subsets from this complete annotation layer rather than recomputing annotations repeatedly. Many-to-many gene-symbol relationships can occur during annotation joins and should be interpreted as a property of the probe-to-gene and gene-set mappings rather than as a preprocessing failure.



5. Save ExpressionSets
Purpose: Persist the normalized, metadata-enriched ExpressionSet objects for reuse by analysis and reporting pipelines.
Output:

output/global_cancer/RData/global_cancer_eset_list.RData

This file serves as the primary R-side preprocessing checkpoint. When build_esets = FALSE, the runner reloads this file instead of rebuilding the ExpressionSets from CEL files.



6. Export Machine-Learning / Latent-Space Inputs
Purpose: Export canonical CSV files from the selected ExpressionSet for Python/Jupyter workflows.

Controlled by: export_ml_inputs
Script: R/preprocessing/export_ml_inputs.R
Current chip: ml_chip_id <- "hu35ksuba"
Current filter: ml_filter_method <- "variance"
Current feature count: ml_top_n <- 3000

This stage defines the canonical interface between the R-based preprocessing pipeline and downstream Python-based modeling workflows. It establishes a fixed, reproducible representation of the expression and metadata space upon which latent-space geometry and complexity analyses are computed.
This stage does not train the VAE and does not perform latent-space analysis. It defines the R-to-Python data contract.
Behavior:

Extract expression values from Biobase::exprs(eset)
Extract phenotype metadata from Biobase::pData(eset)
Align metadata to expression samples using GSM identifiers
Restore CEL-derived sample IDs as canonical sample IDs
Add standardized metadata aliases for notebook compatibility:

sample_id
disease_clean
tissue_clean

Export the full sample-by-feature expression matrix
Select the top ml_top_n probes by variance
Export the variance-filtered sample-by-feature expression matrix
Export the selected feature list and probe variances
Optionally generate a PCA sanity-check plot

Outputs:







File
Description




hu35ksuba_expr_full.csv
Full sample-by-probe expression matrix


hu35ksuba_expr_top3000_variance.csv
Top-3000 variance-filtered matrix used by latent notebooks


hu35ksuba_metadata_aligned.csv
Metadata aligned to expression rows and containing standardized aliases


hu35ksuba_top3000_variance_features.csv
Selected probe IDs, variances, and ranks


hu35ksuba_pca_sanity_top3000_variance.png
PCA sanity-check plot

title: 7  Preprocessing Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Data
text:
Testing and Validation
Recommended staged validation is:

Phase A: Export-only validation
download_enabled <- FALSE
build_esets      <- FALSE
process_metadata <- FALSE
export_ml_inputs <- TRUE
This tests whether existing saved ExpressionSet objects can be loaded and exported successfully.


Phase B: Metadata-regeneration validation
download_enabled <- FALSE
build_esets      <- FALSE
process_metadata <- TRUE
export_ml_inputs <- TRUE
This tests whether metadata attachment and cleaning remain compatible with the ML export contract.


Phase C: Full local rebuild without FTP download
download_enabled <- FALSE
build_esets      <- TRUE
process_metadata <- TRUE
export_ml_inputs <- TRUE
This tests the full local preprocessing workflow while avoiding the slow remote download step.


Phase D: Full reproducibility run
download_enabled <- TRUE
build_esets      <- TRUE
process_metadata <- TRUE
export_ml_inputs <- TRUE
This should be reserved for final validation because it is the slowest and most operationally fragile run mode.
Key validation checks include:
expr <- read.csv("data/global_cancer/processed/ml_inputs/hu35ksuba_expr_top3000_variance.csv", row.names = 1)
meta <- read.csv("data/global_cancer/processed/ml_inputs/hu35ksuba_metadata_aligned.csv")
stopifnot(nrow(expr) == nrow(meta))
stopifnot(all(rownames(expr) == meta$sample_id))
In Python, metadata should be loaded in a way that preserves sample_id as a column:
metadata = pd.read_csv(metadata_path)
metadata = metadata.set_index("sample_id", drop=False)
Using index_col=0 directly on the metadata CSV removes sample_id from metadata.columns and can break downstream notebook checks.

The outputs of the preprocessing pipeline define the structured input space for all downstream analyses. In particular, they establish the feature space (genes/probes) and sample space (annotated tissues and disease states) upon which both complexity/entropy measures and latent-space representations are computed. As such, preprocessing is not merely a technical step but a foundational stage that constrains the interpretation of all subsequent results.

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9 Filtering of Microarray Expression Data
This document describes the role of probe filtering in the global cancer analysis workflow. It should be read together with the preprocessing and analysis pipeline overviews, because filtering in the analysis pipeline is the stage at which the high-dimensional expression matrix is converted into comparison-specific feature sets for downstream complexity, entropy, and biological interpretation tasks.
Latent-space modeling uses a related but distinct feature-selection strategy defined in the preprocessing pipeline, and therefore does not necessarily operate on the same comparison-specific feature sets described here.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.2 Why Filtering Is Required
Gene-expression microarray datasets are high-dimensional but sample-limited. The Ramaswamy dataset contains many thousands of probes, while each tissue-specific normal/tumor comparison contains far fewer samples. Without filtering, downstream calculations would be dominated by probes that contribute little stable information: low signal, low reproducibility, low contrast, inconsistent annotation, or noise-dominated variation.
Filtering is therefore not only a statistical preprocessing step. In this project it has three distinct roles:

it defines the probe space used by traditional complexity and entropy measures;
it provides a comparison-specific feature space that can be contrasted with the global feature-selection strategy used for latent-space modeling;
it shapes the biological interpretation of complexity, entropy, and latent geometry by determining which genes and gene sets are eligible to contribute to the analysis.

These roles are related but not identical. A filter that is appropriate for differential-expression interpretation is not necessarily identical to one that stabilizes geometric structure, and neither necessarily matches the feature-selection regime used for latent-space modeling. For that reason, filtering should be documented as an analytic choice rather than treated as a purely mechanical cleanup step.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.3 Filtering and Traditional Complexity/Entropy Measures
The legacy R-side analysis computes complexity and entropy measures on normal/tumor expression matrices after comparison-specific probe selection. In this setting, filtering defines the empirical state space over which covariance structure, dimensionality, dispersion, and entropy-like quantities are estimated.
Measures such as effective rank, participation ratio, eigenvalue entropy, anisotropy, and related covariance-derived statistics are sensitive to both the number and identity of retained probes. Including many uninformative or noise-dominated probes can inflate apparent dimensionality or obscure coherent structure. Conversely, retaining only highly differential probes can make the analysis more focused but may bias the measured state space toward large normal/tumor contrasts.
For this reason, the pipeline supports two conceptually distinct probe-selection regimes:







Filtering mode
Interpretation for complexity/entropy analysis




limma
Complexity and entropy are assessed within a contrast-enriched probe space defined by differential expression between normal and tumor samples.


variance
Complexity and entropy are assessed within a dispersion-enriched probe space defined by high-variance probes within each comparison.



Results obtained under different filtering regimes should not be interpreted as interchangeable without explicitly considering the effect of the filtering choice.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.4 Filtering and Latent Space
The latent-space workflow reduces high-dimensional gene-expression data to a lower-dimensional representation. Filtering affects this workflow because the model can only learn from the feature set it receives.
In the current project, the latent-space workflow and the R-side complexity/entropy analysis generally operate on different feature universes.
The latent-space pipeline uses a fixed, global set of high-variance probes selected across all samples on a given chip. In contrast, the R-side analysis uses comparison-specific probe selection based on predefined normal/tumor contrasts.
As a result, latent-space representations and complexity/entropy measures are computed in different feature spaces. Direct numerical correspondence between these systems should therefore be interpreted cautiously. Agreement may reflect shared biological structure, whereas disagreement may arise from biological, geometric, or filtering-related differences.
Latent-space representations should therefore be interpreted as embeddings of a filtered transcriptomic subspace rather than of the full gene-expression space.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.5 Filtering and Biological Meaning
Filtering determines the biological vocabulary available for interpretation. GO terms, KEGG pathways, and other gene-set summaries depend on which probes remain after filtering and on how those probes map to annotation resources.
A large retained-probe count in a normal/tumor comparison may indicate broad transcriptional reorganization, but it can also reflect sample size, platform coverage, variance structure, or threshold choice. A small retained-probe count may indicate a more subtle tumor-normal contrast, but it can also reflect low power, heterogeneous samples, conservative thresholds, or platform limitations.
Biological interpretation should therefore be phrased cautiously. Filtered probe sets support statements such as:

the comparison shows a larger or smaller number of probes meeting the configured filter;
the retained probes are enriched for, or annotated to, particular biological functions or pathways;
complexity/entropy or latent-space patterns are being estimated within the retained feature space.

They do not by themselves prove that a tissue has globally gained or lost biological complexity. Rather, they help define the measured transcriptomic subspace in which complexity, entropy, and latent geometry are evaluated.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.6 Limma-Oriented Filtering
The limma option is differential-expression-oriented. In the current configuration, it uses:
filter_method <- "limma"
logfc_cutoff  <- 0.33
pval_cutoff   <- 0.05
In this mode, the retained probes are those that pass the configured normal/tumor contrast criteria. This makes the downstream analysis strongly tied to normal/tumor transcriptional divergence. It is a defensible choice when the primary question is how the structure of expression changes among probes that differ between normal and malignant tissue.
The corresponding interpretive caution is that the measured complexity and entropy are conditional on a differential-expression filter. The results describe the structure of the selected contrast-associated probe set, not the full transcriptome.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.7 Variance-Oriented Filtering
The variance option is structure-oriented. In the current configuration, it is parameterized as:
filter_method <- "variance"
variance_selection_mode <- "top_n"
analysis_top_n <- 3000
The variance filter supports two modes. In top_n mode, a fixed number of high-variance probes is retained per comparison. In threshold mode, probes are retained according to the configured variance threshold:
var_threshold <- 0.75
Retained probes are selected based on variability within each comparison-specific matrix. This can emphasize dominant axes of variation and support geometric interpretation.
The corresponding interpretive caution is that variance is not synonymous with biological relevance. Highly variable probes may reflect tumor biology, tissue identity, cellular mixture, technical effects, or other sources of heterogeneity. For that reason, variance-filtered results should be interpreted together with metadata, annotation, and comparison-specific biological context.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.8 Dynamic Filtering of Gene Sets
In addition to probe-level filtering, the analysis workflow also uses gene-set-level filtering for GO, KEGG, MSigDB, or combined gene-set modes. The current configuration includes:
gene_set_mode   <- "ALL"
quantile_cutoff <- 0.75
min_probes      <- 5
Gene-set filtering is necessary because gene-set sizes are highly uneven. Some pathways contain many probes, while many GO terms contain very few mapped probes. Very small gene sets can produce unstable estimates of complexity, entropy, or enrichment-like summaries. Very large gene sets can dominate interpretation because they represent broad biological categories rather than specific mechanisms.
The goal of dynamic filtering is therefore to retain gene sets that are large enough to support stable calculation while avoiding indiscriminate inclusion of sparse or poorly supported terms.

section: 9.9 Application in the Current Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.9 Application in the Current Analysis Pipeline
Filtering is implemented as Stage 3 of the analysis workflow. The driver script sources the grouped filtering wrapper and high-variance helper, then runs filtering separately for each supported chip. During this stage, the pipeline validates variance_selection_mode, derives the top_n argument only when variance filtering is run in top_n mode, and passes the filtering method, variance-selection mode, and active parameters to the grouped filtering wrapper. The resulting RDS objects include provenance metadata describing the filtering regime used to generate them. The current chips are:








Chip
GEO platform
Role




hu35ksuba
GPL98
One Affymetrix platform used in the Ramaswamy dataset


hu6800
GPL80
Second Affymetrix platform used in the Ramaswamy dataset



The filtering stage is comparison-aware because it operates against predefined normal/tumor comparison maps rather than applying one universal probe list to all comparisons. This is appropriate for the R-side pairwise framework, where each comparison represents a distinct empirical contrast.
The practical output of this stage is not a final biological claim. It is a set of comparison-specific feature lists that define the input space for subsequent complexity, entropy, aggregation, summary, and reporting stages.

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.11 Interpreting Filtered Probe Counts
Filtered probe counts should be interpreted as properties of a configured analysis run. They depend on the selected filtering mode, thresholds, platform, comparison map, and available annotation.
A comparison with many retained probes may reflect broad normal/tumor transcriptional divergence. It may also reflect larger sample size, stronger statistical power, or platform-specific probe coverage. A comparison with few retained probes may reflect a subtler tumor-normal contrast, heterogeneous samples, conservative thresholds, or technical constraints.
For hematologic malignancies, high retained-probe counts may be consistent with strong transcriptional programs related to lineage, proliferation, immune signaling, or differentiation state. For epithelial tumors, lower overlap across carcinoma comparisons may reflect tissue-specific oncogenic programs and differences in cell of origin. These interpretations are plausible but should remain conditional on the filtering method and platform.
crumbs: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline

title: 8  Filtering Strategy for the Global Cancer Analysis Pipeline
text:
9.12 Shared Probes Within Tumor Categories
Shared-probe counts summarize how many filtered probes recur across comparisons within a broad tumor category. They can be useful for identifying repeated transcriptional signals within a category, but they should not be treated as direct measures of biological similarity without considering tissue composition, platform coverage, and comparison design.

Low shared-probe counts suggest either greater biological divergence, stronger tissue specificity, reduced statistical overlap, or platform-dependent limitations. Missing values should not be interpreted as absence of signal; they may reflect absent comparisons or incomplete platform coverage.

title: 9  Analysis Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Structure
text:
Overview
This document describes the analysis pipeline used after preprocessing of the Ramaswamy global cancer dataset. The pipeline consumes normalized, metadata-enriched ExpressionSet objects and full-chip annotation outputs from the preprocessing workflow, constructs tissue-specific comparison matrices, filters probe sets, runs pairwise complexity and entropy analyses, aggregates results, summarizes comparison-level patterns, and optionally executes and postprocesses the latent-space notebooks.
The purpose of this document is to baseline the current analysis workflow. It is therefore primarily descriptive. In particular, the final sections identify the current reporting interface and open questions about which outputs are stable, which are intermediate, and which may need further inspection before changing the reporting layer.
Driver script: scripts/analysis/02_run_analysis_pipeline.R
Configuration file: R/config/global_cancer/analysis_config.R
Primary upstream inputs:
Primary analysis outputs:
Primary latent-space postprocessing outputs:
Primary log file:
The analysis pipeline is the main bridge between the preprocessed expression objects and the downstream reporting layer. It also serves as the integration point between the older R-side complexity/entropy workflow and the newer Python/Jupyter latent-space workflow.
These workflows share upstream data but operate on different feature-selection regimes and therefore different effective feature spaces.

title: 9  Analysis Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Structure
text:
Historical and Methodological Context
The analysis workflow preserves the original dissertation-level aim of comparing normal and malignant expression structure within tissue-specific contrasts. In the current implementation, that aim is operationalized through chip-specific ExpressionSet objects, predefined tissue/tumor comparison maps, comparison-aware probe filtering, pairwise complexity and entropy engines, and gene-set-level annotation.
The pipeline has also been extended to coordinate latent-space analysis. The latent notebooks are executed from the analysis driver only when explicitly enabled, and their outputs can then be postprocessed into tables, plots, and report-facing summary files.
Because the reporting pipeline was developed as a separate abstraction layer, the analysis workflow should be treated as the place where reporting inputs originate or are consolidated. Before changing either pipeline, the practical task is to identify which report-facing objects are produced here, what formats they use, and whether their formats are safe to change.

title: 9  Analysis Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Structure
text:
Pipeline Controls
The pipeline is controlled through analysis_config.R. The principal toggles are:







Toggle
Purpose




build_matrix_maps
Build chip-specific tissue matrices and predefined comparison maps


run_filtering
Run comparison-aware probe selection for each chip


run_pairwise
Run pairwise complexity and entropy comparisons


run_aggregator
Aggregate pairwise engine results and attach gene-set names


run_comparison_summary
Summarize comparison-level complexity and entropy patterns


run_latent_notebooks
Execute the latent-space Jupyter notebooks through nbconvert


run_latent_postprocessing
Build latent-space postprocessing tables, plots, and report-facing outputs



The current analysis configuration includes:
filter_method <- "limma"  # "limma" or "variance"
logfc_cutoff  <- 0.33
pval_cutoff   <- 0.05
var_threshold <- 0.75
top_n         <- 3000 # used for latent-space export (preprocessing), not comparison-level filtering

chips   <- c("hu35ksuba", "hu6800")
engines <- c("complexity", "entropy")

gene_set_mode   <- "ALL"
quantile_cutoff <- 0.75
min_probes      <- 5
The latent-space postprocessing configuration currently uses:
latent_postprocessing_chip_id       <- "hu35ksuba"
latent_postprocessing_mode          <- "ALL"
latent_postprocessing_gene_set_name <- "ALL"
This means that the R-side complexity/entropy workflow is configured for both supported chips, while the current latent postprocessing layer is explicitly focused on hu35ksuba.

title: 9  Analysis Workflow for Global Cancer Expression Structure
text:
Workflow Steps

1. Load Configuration and Start Logging
Purpose: Initialize the analysis run, load paths and toggles, and create the analysis pipeline log.

Configuration: R/config/global_cancer/analysis_config.R
Logger: R/helpers/pipeline_logger.R
Log file: output/global_cancer/logs/analysis/analysis_pipeline_log.txt

The driver script begins by sourcing the configuration file into the global analysis run context. It then starts a pipeline logger and records the beginning of the run.



2. Build Expression Matrix and Comparison Maps
Purpose: Convert preprocessed ExpressionSet objects into tissue-specific expression matrices and predefined normal/tumor comparison maps.

Controlled by: build_matrix_maps
Input: output/global_cancer/RData/global_cancer_eset_list.RData
Helper: R/helpers/matrix_builders.R
Output: output/global_cancer/RData/expression_matrices/global_cancer_matrix_maps.RData

Behavior:

Load eset_list from memory if available
Otherwise load eset_list from the preprocessing checkpoint
Build chip-specific matrix lists by tissue
Define curated comparison maps for each chip, retaining only comparisons whose required normal and tumor matrices are present.
Save the matrix lists and comparison maps as a reusable checkpoint

Objects saved:







Object
Description




matrices_hu35ksuba
Tissue-specific expression matrices for the hu35ksuba chip


matrices_hu6800
Tissue-specific expression matrices for the hu6800 chip


comparison_map_hu35ksuba
Predefined normal/tumor comparison map for hu35ksuba


comparison_map_hu6800
Predefined normal/tumor comparison map for hu6800



This stage defines the empirical state spaces used by the later pairwise comparisons.



3. Run Comparison-Aware Probe Selection
Purpose: Select probe sets for each predefined normal–tumor comparison using the configured probe-selection regime, thereby defining comparison-specific feature spaces for downstream analysis.

Controlled by: run_filtering
Loader: R/helpers/load_results_into_global.R
Wrapper: R/wrappers/run_grouped_probe_filtering.R
Variance helper: R/filters/select_high_variance_probes.R
Outputs:

output/global_cancer/RData/filtered_probes/filtered_probes_hu35ksuba.rds
output/global_cancer/RData/filtered_probes/filtered_probes_hu6800.rds


Current probe-selection regimes:









Regime
Parameters
Intended role
Group sensitivity




limma
logfc_cutoff, pval_cutoff
Differential-expression-driven feature selection
Explicitly normal/tumor-aware


variance, top_n mode
analysis_top_n
Fixed-count high-variance feature selection per comparison
Comparison-aware, not class-label-aware


variance, threshold mode
var_threshold
Quantile-threshold high-variance feature selection per comparison
Comparison-aware, not class-label-aware



In the current configuration, the analysis pipeline uses variance-based probe selection with:
filter_method <- "variance"
variance_selection_mode <- "top_n"
analysis_top_n <- 3000
The pipeline validates variance_selection_mode, derives the top_n argument only when filter_method = "variance" and variance_selection_mode = "top_n", and then passes both top_n and variance_selection_mode into run_grouped_probe_filtering(). If variance_selection_mode = "threshold", top_n is set to NULL and var_threshold is used instead.
The analysis_top_n parameter is distinct from the global top-N feature-selection parameter used for latent-space export during preprocessing. In the present configuration, both may use the value 3000, but they define different feature spaces: analysis_top_n defines local, comparison-specific high-variance probe sets, whereas the latent-space export uses a global chip-level high-variance probe set. The analysis pipeline itself notes that latent-space workflows may operate on a different feature space than the comparison-specific probe sets used here.
Behavior:

Applies probe selection independently for each predefined comparison
Uses comparison maps to assemble the relevant normal and tumor matrices
Produces comparison-specific probe sets rather than a single global feature space
Persists selected probe identifiers for reuse in downstream analysis and reporting stages
Records filtering provenance metadata in the filtered-probe result object, including chip ID, filtering method, variance-selection mode, and active filtering parameters

This stage is comparison-aware: probe selection is performed separately within each predefined tissue-specific normal–tumor contrast. However, the statistical meaning of “selected probes” depends on the configured regime. The limma regime explicitly uses normal/tumor class labels, whereas the variance regime selects high-dispersion probes from the assembled comparison matrix without modeling class labels directly.
These selected probe sets serve as the input to:

complexity and entropy calculations in the legacy R framework
downstream biological interpretation layers, including GO/KEGG summaries
cross-framework comparison with latent-space analyses

These probe sets define comparison-specific feature spaces for the R-side analysis pipeline. In contrast, latent-space workflows typically operate on a fixed, global feature set selected during preprocessing. As a result, the two systems may not be evaluated in identical feature spaces, and comparisons between them should be interpreted accordingly.



4. Run Pairwise Complexity and Entropy Comparisons
Purpose: Apply the configured analysis engines to each supported chip-specific normal/tumor comparison.

Controlled by: run_pairwise
Loader: R/helpers/load_results_into_global.R
Annotation helper: R/helpers/load_annotations.R
Wrapper: R/wrappers/run_all_pairwise_comparisons.R
Engines: complexity, entropy
Gene-set modes: ALL, GO_BP, GO_MF, KEGG, MSIGDB

Inputs:

Matrix/comparison-map checkpoint from Stage 2
Filtered-probe objects from Stage 3
Full-chip annotations from preprocessing

Behavior:

Load expression matrices and comparison maps
Load filtered probe results
Load full-chip annotation layer if needed
Run all pairwise comparisons for configured chips, engines, and gene-set modes

This stage is where the R-side biological complexity and entropy analyses are computed. The exact output layout is produced by the pairwise-comparison wrapper and downstream engine functions; these files are later collected by the aggregation stage.



5. Aggregate Engine Results and Attach Gene-Set Names
Purpose: Consolidate pairwise engine outputs into cleaned aggregate tables for complexity and entropy.

Controlled by: run_aggregator
Loader: R/helpers/load_results_into_global.R
Aggregator: R/aggregate/aggregate_engine_results_by_engine.R
Gene-set helper: R/helpers/gene_set_tools.R
Cleaning helper: R/helpers/clean_aggregated_results.R
Outputs:

output/global_cancer/RData/aggregated/complexity_aggregated_results.csv
output/global_cancer/RData/aggregated/complexity_aggregated_results.rds
output/global_cancer/RData/aggregated/entropy_aggregated_results.csv
output/global_cancer/RData/aggregated/entropy_aggregated_results.rds


Behavior:

Load pairwise comparison results from the analysis data directory
Ensure the annotation layer is available
Aggregate results separately for the complexity and entropy engines
Attach gene-set names from the annotation layer
Clean the aggregate tables
Save both CSV and RDS forms of each aggregate table

This stage is a major candidate source for downstream reporting tables because it produces explicit tabular complexity and entropy result files.



6. Summarize Comparison-Level Patterns
Purpose: Collapse aggregated complexity and entropy outputs into comparison-level summaries.

Controlled by: run_comparison_summary
Loader: R/helpers/load_results_into_global.R
Complexity summary: R/summarize/summarize_complexity.R
Entropy summary: R/summarize/summarize_entropy.R
Summary combiner: R/summarize/combine_entropy_complexity_summaries.R
Outputs:

output/global_cancer/RData/summaries/summaries_combined_df.csv
output/global_cancer/RData/summaries/summaries_combined_df.rds


Behavior:

Load cleaned aggregate complexity and entropy results
Recalculate the complexity summary table
Recalculate the entropy summary table
Combine both summaries into a unified comparison-level table
Save both CSV and RDS forms of the combined summary table

This summary file appears to represent one of the most compact R-side outputs for comparison-level interpretation.



7. Execute Latent-Space Notebooks (Optional)
Purpose: Optionally execute the Python/Jupyter latent-space workflow from the R analysis driver.

Controlled by: run_latent_notebooks
Notebook command: jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute
Notebook directory: projects/cancer-latent-space/notebooks
Optional executed-copy directory: output/global_cancer/notebooks/executed

Configured notebooks:







Notebook
Role




00_latent_space_config.ipynb
Shared latent-space paths, configuration, and abbreviations


01_load_and_inspect_data.ipynb
Load and inspect exported ML inputs


02_prepare_data_for_vae.ipynb
Prepare expression data for VAE modeling


03_train_first_vae.ipynb
Train the initial VAE


04_latent_space_analysis.ipynb
Join latent coordinates to metadata and inspect latent structure


05_latent_complexity_analysis.ipynb
Compute within-comparison latent complexity measures


06_between_cancer_geometry_in_latent_space_enhanced.ipynb
Compute between-cancer latent geometry measures



When execute_notebooks_inplace is TRUE, notebook outputs are written back into the notebooks themselves. When FALSE, executed copies are written to the configured output directory.
This stage does not itself define the latent-space methodology; it orchestrates notebook execution from within the R analysis pipeline.
The latent-space workflow operates on feature sets defined during preprocessing (typically global high-variance probes), which may differ from the comparison-specific feature spaces used by the R-side analysis pipeline. As a result, latent-space metrics and R-side complexity/entropy measures are not necessarily computed on identical feature spaces.



8. Run Latent-Space Postprocessing
Purpose: Build R-side latent-space postprocessing outputs from the latent notebook results.

Controlled by: run_latent_postprocessing
Script: R/latent-space/build_latent_postprocessing_outputs.R
Table output directory: output/global_cancer/tables/latent/
Plot output directory: output/global_cancer/plots/latent/
Report output directory: output/global_cancer/reports/

Current settings:
latent_postprocessing_chip_id       <- "hu35ksuba"
latent_postprocessing_mode          <- "ALL"
latent_postprocessing_gene_set_name <- "ALL"
This stage is especially relevant to the reporting pipeline because it appears to produce table, plot, and report-facing outputs after the Jupyter notebooks have completed. It should be inspected together with the reporting pipeline before any assumption is made about whether the current report layer consumes structured files, rendered fragments, generated QMD content, or a mixture of these.

text:
Reporting Interface and Open Questions
The current PDF/HTML rendering issue should be investigated by tracing report inputs back to the analysis outputs. At this stage, the appropriate goal is not to prescribe a replacement format, but to document the existing interface.
Key questions for review:







Question
Why it matters




Which reporting tables are generated directly from complexity_aggregated_results.* and entropy_aggregated_results.*?
These are structured outputs and may be easier to render consistently across HTML and PDF


Which reporting tables are generated from summaries_combined_df.*?
This file may be the cleanest comparison-level summary source


Which reporting tables are produced by latent-space postprocessing?
The latent postprocessing stage appears to create report-facing artifacts after notebook execution


Are any GO/KEGG/MSigDB tables persisted as rendered HTML rather than data?
This may explain HTML/PDF rendering divergence, but should be confirmed before changing code


Are generated QMD files reading data frames, CSV/RDS files, HTML fragments, or preassembled text blocks?
This determines whether the issue belongs in analysis, latent postprocessing, or reporting


Which outputs are relied on by the reporting pipeline exactly as written?
Some apparent intermediate files may be necessary for the existing abstraction layer


Which output formats can be changed without breaking generated reports?
This defines the safe scope for future refactoring



Recommended audit path:

Inspect R/latent-space/build_latent_postprocessing_outputs.R.
Inspect the reporting pipeline for references to output/global_cancer/RData/aggregated/.
Inspect the reporting pipeline for references to output/global_cancer/RData/summaries/.
Inspect the reporting pipeline for references to output/global_cancer/tables/latent/ and output/global_cancer/reports/.
Identify whether failing GO/KEGG tables originate from aggregate CSV/RDS data, generated QMD code, or pre-rendered HTML.
Only then decide whether changes belong in analysis, latent postprocessing, reporting, or the QMD template layer.

title: 10  Reporting Workflow for Global Cancer Complexity Analysis
text:
Overview
This document describes the reporting pipeline used to transform aggregated complexity, entropy, and pairwise-comparison results into human-readable summary files, generated comparison-specific Quarto source files, optional GO-clustering tables, and final rendered report outputs.
The reporting pipeline is intentionally downstream of the preprocessing and analysis pipelines. It does not rebuild ExpressionSet objects, recompute probe annotations, or rerun the core entropy/complexity calculations. Instead, it loads previously generated analysis products and converts them into report-ready artifacts.
Driver script: scripts/reporting/03_run_report_pipeline.R
Configuration file: R/config/global_cancer/report_config.R
Primary input directories:
Primary report-output directory:
Primary Quarto/reporting resources:
Primary log file:
The goal of this reporting pipeline is to create a stable reporting layer on top of the analysis outputs. Its main responsibilities are to load previously saved summary and pairwise-comparison objects, generate cleaned summary artifacts, create comparison-specific Quarto files from templates, optionally generate GO-clustering tables, and optionally render the Quarto book/site.

title: 10  Reporting Workflow for Global Cancer Complexity Analysis
text:
Workflow Steps

1. Load Reporting Configuration and Start Logging
Purpose: Initialize paths, toggles, and the reporting log.

Configuration file: R/config/global_cancer/report_config.R
Logger helper: R/helpers/pipeline_logger.R
Log file: output/global_cancer/logs/reports/reports_pipeline_log.txt

The configuration file defines the study identifier, local output paths, input RDS paths, and reporting toggles. The driver script then starts a dedicated reporting log before loading downstream results.



2. Load Analysis Outputs into the Reporting Environment
Purpose: Load the serialized outputs created by the analysis pipeline.

Helper script: R/helpers/load_results_into_global.R

The driver calls the following helper functions:
load_summaries_df(summaries_dir, overwrite = TRUE)
load_cleaned_results(aggregate_dir, overwrite = TRUE)
load_filtered_results(filtered_probes_dir, overwrite = TRUE)
These calls populate the reporting environment with the objects required by later stages, including:







Object
Description




summaries_combined_df
Combined pairwise-comparison summary table


entropy_df
Cleaned/aggregated entropy results


complexity_df
Cleaned/aggregated complexity results


res_hu35ksuba
Filtered-probe results for the hu35ksuba platform


res_hu6800
Filtered-probe results for the hu6800 platform



This stage assumes that the analysis pipeline has already written the required RDS files to output/global_cancer/RData/.



3. Generate Summary Reports
Purpose: Create text summaries and cleaned report-level copies of the entropy and complexity result tables.

Controlled by: run_summary_reports
Script: R/reports/summarize_pairwise_results.R

The pipeline summarizes entropy and complexity results separately:
entropy_report    <- summarize_pairwise_results(entropy_df, engine = "entropy")
complexity_report <- summarize_pairwise_results(complexity_df, engine = "complexity")
Outputs:







File
Description




output/global_cancer/reports/entropy_summary.txt
Plain-text entropy summary


output/global_cancer/reports/complexity_summary.txt
Plain-text complexity summary


output/global_cancer/reports/entropy_cleaned.csv
CSV copy of cleaned entropy results


output/global_cancer/reports/complexity_cleaned.csv
CSV copy of cleaned complexity results


output/global_cancer/reports/entropy_cleaned.rds
RDS copy of cleaned entropy results


output/global_cancer/reports/complexity_cleaned.rds
RDS copy of cleaned complexity results



The current driver also renders:
R/reports/pairwise_summary_report.Rmd
This appears to be a legacy or parallel R Markdown summary report. It should be retained unless and until the Quarto reporting layer fully replaces it.



4. Generate Comparison-Specific Quarto Files
Purpose: Create one .qmd file per pairwise comparison from a report template.

Controlled by: run_reports
Wrapper: R/wrappers/run_all_reports.R

The driver passes the major reporting objects into run_all_reports():
run_all_reports(
  summaries_combined_df = summaries_combined_df,
  complexity_df = complexity_df,
  entropy_df = entropy_df,
  res_hu35ksuba = res_hu35ksuba,
  res_hu6800 = res_hu6800
)
This stage is responsible for generating comparison-level Quarto source files, not for rendering the final book. The source files are expected to be consumed later by the Quarto project under quarto/.
Conceptual output:

comparison-specific .qmd files, typically under quarto/reports/generated_reports/

Important distinction:
Despite the name run_reports, this stage primarily creates report source files. The actual Quarto rendering step is controlled separately by run_quarto.



5. Run GO-Clustering Analysis
Purpose: Generate clustered GO-term tables for report integration.

Controlled by: run_go_clustering
Wrapper: R/wrappers/run_go_clustering.R
Current output base: quarto/resources/tables/clusters/
Current GO mode: GO_BP

The driver calls:
run_go_clustering_main(
  summaries_combined_df = summaries_combined_df,
  complexity_df = complexity_df,
  entropy_df = entropy_df,
  output_dir_base = here::here("quarto", "resources", "tables", "clusters"),
  go_mode = go_mode
)
This stage is currently disabled by default in report_config.R:
run_go_clustering <- FALSE
When enabled, it should be treated as a biological-interpretation support layer, not as a replacement for the primary entropy and complexity summaries.



6. Generate GO Visualizations
Purpose: Placeholder for GO visualization code.
This section is currently inactive. The driver contains commented code for a GO DAG plotting function:
# source("R/visualizations/plot_entropy_go_dag.R")
# plot_entropy_go_dag("PB/B-ALL")
The configuration file also contains:
run_visualization <- FALSE
At present, visualization code should be considered outside the active reporting pipeline. It can be reintroduced later after the table-generation and Quarto rendering layers are stable.



7. Render the Quarto Project
Purpose: Render the Quarto book/site from the generated source files.

Controlled by: run_quarto
Working directory during render: quarto/

When enabled, the driver temporarily changes into the Quarto directory, removes Quarto’s _freeze directory, and renders the project:
old <- getwd()
setwd(here::here("quarto"))
unlink("_freeze", recursive = TRUE, force = TRUE)
quarto::quarto_render(".")
setwd(old)
The current configuration disables this step by default:
run_quarto <- FALSE
This is a conservative default. It allows the pipeline to regenerate source files and summary tables without forcing a full Quarto render on every reporting run.
